title: Adam Smith's Self-Interest Breakthrough
body:
## Adam Smith's Self-Interest Breakthrough

In 1776 Adam Smith published _The Wealth of Nations_ and destroyed centuries of economic confusion with one sentence:

**"It is not from the benevolence of the butcher, the brewer, or the baker that we expect our dinner, but from their regard to their own interest."**

This was revolutionary.

Before Smith, people thought:

- Trade is zero-sum (one side wins, one loses)
- Merchants are greedy parasites
- Only government planning can create prosperity

Smith showed the opposite:

**Self-interest + free exchange = mutual benefit.**

The butcher doesn't feed you out of charity. He feeds you because _he wants your money_. You give him money because _you want his meat_. Both win.

No king commanded it. No bureaucrat planned it. No one needed to be "good."

The invisible hand of the market turned private self-interest into public prosperity—automatically.

This insight launched modern economics and justified free markets forever.

Socialists still can't refute it. They just pretend self-interest disappears when you give people government power.

It doesn't.

title: America's Classical Liberal Era
body:
## America's Classical Liberal Era

The Founders distilled English liberty into plain words:

- Government only stops harm to others
- Power divided so ambition checks ambition
- Property protected above all

For decades the federal government stayed tiny (<2% GDP). Result: from 13 colonies to the world's richest nation in record time.

Tocqueville marveled in 1831: Americans fixed problems themselves—churches, lodges, companies—not by begging the state.

Low taxes, sound money, open borders, minimal regulation = the greatest prosperity surge in history.

title: Bastiat's Government Paradox
body:
## Bastiat's Government Paradox

Bastiat's devastating one-liner (1850):

"The state is that great fiction by which everyone tries to live at the expense of everyone else."

And the perfect follow-up:

People say: "We don't want the government to grow grain."

Very well.

But then they add: "We want the government to feed the people, create jobs, give free healthcare, subsidise art, guarantee loans…"

It is as if they said:

"We do not want the government to grow grain—yet we demand that it magically provide bread for all."

Government has no resources of its own. It can only take from Peter to give to Paul.

Every "service" is paid for by someone who never consented.

Bastiat exposed the illusion in a single paragraph that still stings 175 years later.

title: Bastiat's Seen and Unseen
body:
## Bastiat's Seen and Unseen

In 1850 Frédéric Bastiat wrote the clearest parable in all economics:

A shopkeeper's son breaks a window.

The crowd says: "At least the glazier gets work. What's bad for the shopkeeper is good for trade!"

Bastiat replies:

That is what is **seen**.

What is **not seen** is the new suit the shopkeeper would have bought instead. The tailor loses work. Net result: society is poorer by one window and one suit.

Every government "stimulus," every regulation, every tax creates visible winners and invisible losers.

Politicians always point to the seen (jobs "created"). Classical liberals ask about the unseen (jobs destroyed, growth prevented).

Bastiat's one short essay destroys 99 % of bad economic thinking—then and now.

title: The Berlin Wall Falls 1989
body:
## The Berlin Wall Falls 1989

On November 9, 1989, the Berlin Wall fell.

It wasn't just a wall. It was the ultimate test:

**Which system works—capitalism or socialism?**

Same people. Same culture. Same history.

One side got freedom and markets.

The other got central planning and secret police.

The results:

**West Berlin**: Prosperity, innovation, freedom. People had cars, food, hope.

**East Berlin**: Poverty, surveillance, despair. People risked death to escape.

The wall wasn't built to keep capitalists out.

It was built to keep socialists _in_.

When it fell, the world saw what socialists had hidden for 28 years:

- Empty stores
- Crumbling buildings
- Stasi files on millions of citizens
- A system so broken that people would rather die than stay

The fall of the Berlin Wall didn't just end the Cold War.

It ended the argument.

Socialism lost. Freedom won. Case closed.

title: Bill of Rights: Limits on Government
grift: Bill of Rights: Limits on Government
body:
## Bill of Rights: Limits on Government

In 1791 the Bill of Rights was ratified.

Most people misunderstand it.

The Bill of Rights doesn't _grant_ rights. It _protects_ rights you already have.

**It tells government what it cannot do.**

Read the language:

- "Congress shall make no law..." (1st Amendment)
- "...shall not be infringed" (2nd Amendment)
- "...shall not be violated" (4th Amendment)

These aren't permissions. They're prohibitions.

The Founders knew that government is the greatest threat to liberty.

So they built a fence around our rights:

**1st Amendment**: Government can't control speech, religion, press, or assembly.

**2nd Amendment**: Government can't disarm the people.

**4th Amendment**: Government can't search you without cause.

**5th Amendment**: Government can't take your property or force self-incrimination.

**10th Amendment**: Powers not given to the federal government belong to states and people.

The pattern is clear: The Bill of Rights limits government, not citizens.

The left treats rights as gifts from government that can be regulated, limited, or revoked.

The Founders knew better: Rights are inherent. Government's job is to protect them—not grant them.

When government claims the power to give rights, it claims the power to take them away.

The Bill of Rights says: No.

title: Edmund Burke Saves Freedom
body:
## Edmund Burke Saves Freedom

Edmund Burke supported the American Revolution and Catholic rights, but in 1790 he saw terror coming in France.

Revolutionaries were destroying church, monarchy, provinces, guilds—even the calendar—in the name of pure Reason.

Burke predicted the guillotine and Napoleon years before they happened. He was mocked, then proven right.

Freedom, he taught, grows slowly in real soil—manners, religion, property, custom. Rip it out and you get tyranny wearing liberty's mask.

Modern conservatism starts here.

title: Chile Miracle 1975–1990
body:
## Chile Miracle 1975–1990

1973 Chile under Allende: 600% inflation, empty shops, expropriations, Marxist chaos.

After Pinochet, the "Chicago Boys" launched radical free-market reforms:

- Privatized pensions, mines, utilities
- Flat 10% income tax
- Opened trade, slashed tariffs
- Independent central bank

1975–1990: average 7% annual growth. Poverty crashed from 45% to 15% by 2000.

Even left-wing governments after 1990 kept the model.

Chile leapt from socialist ruin to Latin America's richest, most stable economy—proof the formula works anywhere.

title: Corn Laws Repealed 1846
body:
## Corn Laws Repealed 1846

For decades the Corn Laws kept foreign grain out and bread prices high for the poor.

Cobden, Bright, and classical liberals fought. In 1846 Conservative PM Robert Peel finally repealed them.

The aristocracy predicted ruin.

Instead:

- Bread prices fell 25–40 %
- Real wages soared
- British farms grew richer on meat and dairy

Britain fed Europe while its own people doubled and prospered.

One vote proved free trade makes everyone richer.

title: Estonia Flat Tax Revolution 1994
body:
## Estonia Flat Tax Revolution 1994

In 1991 Estonia broke free from the Soviet Union—bankrupt, broken, and occupied for 50 years.

Three years later, they did something radical:

**They adopted a 26% flat tax.**

One rate. No deductions. No loopholes. No complexity.

The tax code fit on one page.

Economists said it was reckless. "You need progressive taxation! You need complexity! You need to soak the rich!"

Estonia ignored them.

The results:

- GDP growth averaged 7% per year
- Foreign investment flooded in
- Government revenue _increased_ (because people actually paid)
- Estonia became the "Baltic Tiger"

By 2005, Estonia had the fastest-growing economy in Europe.

Why did it work?

**Simple taxes = less evasion, less corruption, more growth.**

When everyone pays the same rate, there's no incentive to cheat, no army of lobbyists writing loopholes, and no politicians picking winners.

Today, over 40 countries have adopted flat taxes.

Estonia proved that simplicity beats complexity, and freedom beats central planning.

Even after 50 years of Soviet destruction.

title: Federalist 10: Factions and the Republic
body:
## Federalist 10: Factions and the Republic

In 1787 James Madison wrote Federalist No. 10 and solved a problem that had plagued humanity for millennia:

**How do you stop factions from destroying freedom?**

A faction is any group united by passion or interest adverse to the rights of others.

History showed that factions always win:

- In pure democracies, the majority faction tyrannizes the minority
- In small republics, demagogues seize power
- In monarchies, the king's faction oppresses everyone

Madison's solution was brilliant:

**Don't eliminate factions. Control their effects.**

How? Through a large republic with:

1. **Representation** (filters passion through elected officials)
2. **Extended territory** (makes it harder for one faction to dominate)
3. **Diverse interests** (prevents any single faction from forming a majority)

The genius: In a large, diverse republic, factions compete and check each other.

No single group can tyrannize the rest.

This is why America isn't a pure democracy—it's a constitutional republic.

Madison knew that democracy = mob rule.

A republic = rule of law that protects individual rights.

The left pushes "democracy" as the highest good.

Madison knew better: Unchecked democracy destroys liberty.

The Constitution protects us from it.

title: Federalist 51: Ambition Checks Ambition
body:
## Federalist 51: Ambition Checks Ambition

In 1788 James Madison wrote Federalist No. 51 and explained the genius of the Constitution in one line:

**"Ambition must be made to counteract ambition."**

The problem: How do you limit government power when the people running government want more power?

Madison's answer: Don't rely on virtue. Rely on structure.

**Make the branches compete against each other.**

The system:

- Congress makes laws (but the President can veto)
- President enforces laws (but Congress controls the purse)
- Courts interpret laws (but can't enforce them)
- Senate checks the House
- States check the federal government

Each branch has its own powers and its own incentives to defend them.

The result: Gridlock by design.

Madison knew this would frustrate people. That was the point.

**"If men were angels, no government would be necessary."**

But men aren't angels. So you need a system where:

- Power is divided
- Ambition checks ambition
- No one can accumulate enough power to tyrannize

The left hates this. They want "efficient" government that can "get things done."

Madison knew better: Efficient tyranny is still tyranny.

Separation of powers protects liberty—even when it's inconvenient.

title: Milton Friedman's Free to Choose
body:
## Milton Friedman's Free to Choose

In 1980 Milton Friedman released a 10-part PBS series that did the impossible:

**He made free-market economics entertaining.**

Friedman took his camera crew everywhere:

- Hong Kong (freest economy, fastest growth)
- India (socialist disaster)
- Scotland (Adam Smith's birthplace)
- American schools (government monopoly failure)

Each episode demolished a leftist myth:

**Episode 1**: The power of the market creates prosperity, not government plans.

**Episode 5**: Created equal means equal rights, not equal outcomes.

**Episode 9**: Government cure is worse than the disease.

His message was simple:

Freedom works. Coercion fails. Every time.

The book became a bestseller. The series reached millions. Reagan quoted him constantly.

Friedman proved you could win the argument _and_ win the audience.

Socialists have never recovered.

title: Glorious Revolution 1688–89
body:
## Glorious Revolution 1688–89

In 1688 England stood on the edge of absolutist monarchy. James II was building a standing army, suspending laws by royal decree, and trying to re-impose Catholicism by force.

A group of bishops and nobles invited the Dutch prince William of Orange to invade and rescue English liberty.

William landed with a fleet larger than the Spanish Armada—yet almost no blood was shed. James II fled. Parliament passed the **English Bill of Rights (1689)**:

- No taxation without consent
- Free speech in Parliament
- No standing army in peacetime without Parliament's approval
- Right to petition the king
- Right of Protestants to bear arms

For the first time, a great nation declared that government exists to protect pre-existing rights. The state became a "night watchman." Classical Liberalism was born.

title: The Use of Knowledge in Society
grift: The Use of Knowledge in Society
body:
## The Use of Knowledge in Society

Published in the American Economic Review, September 1945.

This is the essay everyone quotes when they say "the knowledge-problem argument" or "Hayek's knowledge problem."

Key lines that changed economics forever:

- "The economic problem of society is not merely a problem of how to allocate 'given' resources… it is a problem of the **utilization of knowledge which is not given to anyone in its totality**."

- Most of the knowledge we need to coordinate a modern economy is **not** scientific data in textbooks.

It is local, fleeting, tacit:

- a factory manager knows his machines are wearing out

- a trader knows copper is suddenly scarce in one port

- a housewife knows she prefers more fruit this month

- No central planner can ever collect this dispersed, changing, often unarticulated knowledge.

- The price system solves it automatically: a higher copper price instantly tells thousands of producers and users around the world "economise or find substitutes" — without anyone needing to know _why_ the price rose.

Hayek's conclusion is devastating for central planning:

"The marvel is that in a case like that of the scarcity of one raw material, without an order being issued, without more than perhaps a few people knowing the cause, tens of thousands of people… are made to use the material or its products more sparingly; that is, they move in the right direction."

Mises showed socialism can't calculate.

Hayek showed that even if it somehow could, it still couldn't **discover** what needs to be calculated.

This 1945 essay (just 11 pages) is the single most cited and most lethal attack on central planning ever written.

title: Hayek's Road to Serfdom 1944
body:
## Hayek's Road to Serfdom 1944

In 1944, while the world fought Nazis and Soviets, Friedrich Hayek warned the West was walking the same path.

Central planning—even with good intentions—kills freedom.

Why? Plans need coercion. Someone must allocate resources. Dissent threatens the plan, so dissent is crushed.

Published in 1944, the book predicted nationalization → price controls → shortages → dictatorship.

Britain's 1945–51 Labour government followed the script almost perfectly.

Hayek proved socialism and freedom cannot coexist. A bestseller that later armed Thatcher, Reagan, and millions.

title: Hayek's Fatal Conceit 1988
body:
## Hayek's Fatal Conceit 1988

In 1988, at age 89, Friedrich Hayek dropped his final bombshell: _The Fatal Conceit: The Errors of Socialism_.

Socialism's core mistake is the belief that a few minds can consciously design a better order than the spontaneous order that billions create through free choices.

Civilisation is not the product of reason—it is the product of the extended order of markets, morals, language, law, and money that evolved over millennia.

This order is far wiser than any planner because it uses knowledge no single brain can hold: prices signal scarcity, traditions encode survival lessons, competition weeds out bad ideas.

To replace it with central planning is the height of arrogance—the "fatal conceit."

Hayek wrote it as Communism was visibly dying. Three years later the Soviet Union was gone.

The 20th century's greatest experiment in rational design ended in bread lines and mass graves. The unplanned order of liberty won again.

title: Hong Kong Miracle 1950-1997
body:
## Hong Kong Miracle 1950-1997

In 1950 Hong Kong was a barren rock with no natural resources and a million refugees fleeing communist China.

By 1997 it was one of the richest places on Earth.

What happened?

**The freest economy in the world.**

Hong Kong's formula was radical:

- No tariffs
- No capital controls
- Minimal regulation
- Low, simple taxes
- Rule of law
- Property rights

The government's role: Stay out of the way.

The results were staggering:

- GDP per capita grew 180-fold
- Life expectancy jumped from 60 to 80
- A fishing village became a global financial center

Milton Friedman called it "the best example we have of a free-market economy."

Socialists said it couldn't work. "You need planning! You need industrial policy! You need government investment!"

Hong Kong proved them wrong.

Freedom + property rights + rule of law = prosperity.

Every. Single. Time.

Then China took over in 1997 and started strangling it.

The lesson remains: Freedom works. Socialism destroys.

title: Liberalism Isn't dead - It's Buried Under 120 Years of Collectivist Garbage
grift: Liberalism Isn't dead - It's Buried Under 120 Years of Collectivist Garbage
body:
## Liberalism Isn’t dead, don't confuse it with Leftism.

![Declaration of Iindependence painting](/posts/declaration-of-independence-painting.jpg)

Liberalism is just buried under 120 years of collectivist garbage.

The core of liberalism is dead-simple:

- You own your life
- You own your body
- You own the fruits of your labor
- You get to speak, worship, assemble, and trade with whomever you want
- The government’s only job is to stop other people from violating those rights
- Equal rules for everyone, no special privileges for no one

That’s it. Everything else is commentary.

From roughly 1688 (Glorious Revolution) to 1914, the parts of the world that actually practiced this (Britain, the Netherlands, the United States, etc.) gave mankind the single greatest explosion of freedom, wealth, invention, and living standards in recorded history.

A very partial list of what freedom delivered in one century:

railroads · steamships · telegraph · telephone · electric light & power · recorded sound · photography · motion pictures · automobiles · airplanes · radio · X-rays · quantum physics · germ theory · anesthesia · antiseptics · the periodic table · skyscrapers · mass steel & aluminum · aspirin · diesel engines · indoor plumbing for the masses · real wages up 500–1000 % for ordinary workers · life expectancy from ~30 to ~70

Name another system that comes within an order of magnitude of that scorecard. I’ll wait.

Then the Marxists showed up and said “Equality before the law isn’t enough — you need enforced equality of outcome, and we’ll deliver it at gunpoint.”

Liberalism, because it actually believes in free speech and tolerance, let them talk.
**Big mistake.**

Gramsci told them to march through the institutions instead of the streets.
They did.

We laughed at the bearded weirdos in 1968.
Their students now run the universities, the NGOs, the HR departments, the media, and half the government bureaucracies.

We beat the USSR but imported the ideology anyway.

Today, the idea that the government should leave you alone is considered “far-right.”
That is how completely the Overton window has been dragged left.

In 1900, a 90 % top marginal tax rate and the state running your healthcare, education, and retirement would have been considered literal tyranny.
In 2025, suggesting we get rid of those things is considered literal tyranny.

That’s not “liberalism failed.”
That’s “we stopped being liberals and became soft-socialists while pretending we didn’t.”

And yeah, classical liberals in the 19th century were generally pro-free-migration because most countries still had small welfare states and tiny governments. Times have changed — massive vote-buying welfare states plus open borders is a suicide pact, not a liberal policy. So we adapt that part. The rest of the program is still perfect.

**Bottom line:**
I don’t defend liberalism because it created the wealthiest, healthiest, most innovative century in human history (even though it did).

I defend it because it is the **only system** that treats you like a free, adult human being instead of a toddler who needs the state to wipe your nose and plan your life.

Anyone arguing against maximum freedom is, whether they admit it or not, arguing for some flavor of imposed control.
That’s the Left, full stop.

**Freedom > everything else.**
And it just so happens the freest societies also became the richest, safest, and coolest.
Funny how that works.

Want the academic version?
Watch this:\
[Deirdre McCloskey – Why the 19th Century Boom Happened](https://youtu.be/a0nsKBx77EQ)

title: Magna Carta 1215
body:
## Magna Carta 1215

In 1215, English barons forced tyrannical King John to sign the Great Charter at Runnymede.

Key clauses:

- No taxation without consent
- No imprisonment without lawful judgment
- Justice cannot be sold or delayed

Originally for nobles, it became the seed of liberty: even the king is under the law.

Parliament cited it for centuries to expand habeas corpus, trial by jury, and limits on royal power. Classical liberals from Coke to Blackstone called it England's ancient constitution.

The first written curb on absolute power in European history—still law in 2025.

title: Mainstream Media Hoaxes
body:
## How many recent mainstream media hoaxes did you fall for?

- Russian collusion
- 2020 Election most secure in history
- Trump called neo-nazis "fine people"
- If you get vaccinated you won't catch COVID
- Jussie Smollett
- Bubba Wallace garage pull

- January 6th was an insurrection
- Trump mocked a reporter's disability

title: Mises Crushes Socialism 1922
body:
## Mises Crushes Socialism 1922

In 1922, when almost every intellectual believed scientific socialism was the future, Ludwig von Mises published _Socialism_ and delivered the knockout blow.

His argument was devastatingly simple:

Without private property in the means of production, there are no real market prices.

Without real prices, there is no way to calculate what anything costs or is worth.

Without economic calculation, rational production becomes impossible.

Socialism isn't evil—it is literally impossible. Central planners cannot do the math that millions of free people do automatically every day through buying and selling.

The Soviet Union spent seventy years proving Mises right: endless shortages, misallocated factories, tractors built with no wheels, mountains of unsold goods nobody wanted.

Mises fired the shot in 1922. By 1991 the entire socialist world was bankrupt and gone. The calculation argument won.

title: New Zealand Reforms 1984-1993
body:
## New Zealand Reforms 1984-1993

By 1984 New Zealand was dying.

Decades of socialist policies had turned a prosperous nation into a basket case:

- Massive deficits
- 15% unemployment
- Inflation at 17%
- Price controls on everything
- Import licenses for bananas

The government controlled wages, prices, interest rates, and foreign exchange.

Then Labour Finance Minister Roger Douglas did the unthinkable:

**He unleashed capitalism.**

The reforms were brutal and fast:

- Slashed tariffs from 40% to 5%
- Eliminated subsidies
- Privatized state industries
- Deregulated everything
- Cut top tax rate from 66% to 33%
- Floated the currency

Unions screamed. Socialists rioted. The left called it "Rogernomics" as an insult.

The results:

- Inflation crushed to 2%
- Budget balanced
- Economy grew 4% per year
- Unemployment fell
- New Zealand became competitive again

The lesson: Even a socialist government can save a nation—if it abandons socialism.

Douglas proved that free markets work even when socialists implement them.

title: No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics
grift: No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics
body:
## There is No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics.

"In addition, those of us who support such [so-called Trickle down] policies don’t want to “give” anything to anyone, whether rich or poor. When people talk about tax cuts as “giving” something to someone, they implicitly start from the premise that everything belongs to government and we are only able to keep some for ourselves by its indulgence of us." - Steven Horwitz

[FEE article - There is No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics](https://fee.org/articles/there-is-no-such-thing-as-trickle-down-economics/ 'There is No Such Thing as Trickle-Down Economics')

title: Property Rights Equal Prosperity
body:
## Property Rights Equal Prosperity

Peruvian economist Hernando de Soto asked a simple question:

**Why are some nations rich and others poor?**

His answer shocked the development world:

**Property rights.**

In his book _The Mystery of Capital_ (2000), de Soto showed that the poor aren't poor because they lack assets.

They're poor because they lack _legal title_ to their assets.

In Peru, a street vendor owns his cart. A farmer owns her land. But they have no legal deed.

Without legal title:

- You can't use it as collateral for a loan
- You can't sell it easily
- You can't prove you own it
- You can't build wealth

De Soto calculated that the world's poor hold over $10 trillion in "dead capital"—assets they own but can't leverage.

The solution isn't foreign aid or government programs.

It's **clear, enforceable property rights.**

When people can legally own, buy, and sell property:

- Capital flows
- Credit expands
- Investment grows
- Prosperity follows

This is why the West got rich: Property rights + rule of law.

This is why the Third World stays poor: Corruption + unclear ownership.

The left blames "capitalism" for poverty.

De Soto proved the opposite: Lack of capitalism causes poverty.

title: Public Choice Theory: Politicians Aren't Angels
grift: Public Choice Theory: Politicians Aren't Angels
body:
## Public Choice Theory: Politicians Aren't Angels

In 1962 James Buchanan and Gordon Tullock published _The Calculus of Consent_ and destroyed a myth that had ruled for centuries:

**Politicians are just as self-interested as everyone else.**

Before Public Choice Theory, economists assumed:

- Markets fail because people are greedy
- Government fixes markets because politicians are noble
- Bureaucrats serve "the public interest"

Buchanan asked one question:

**Why would self-interest disappear when someone enters government?**

It doesn't.

Politicians want re-election. Bureaucrats want bigger budgets. Interest groups want subsidies.

The result: Government "solutions" that benefit politicians and special interests—not the public.

Examples everywhere:

- Farm subsidies (enriching agribusiness, not feeding the poor)
- Occupational licensing (protecting incumbents, not consumers)
- Tariffs (helping connected industries, hurting everyone else)

Public Choice Theory explains why government fails:

**Not because politicians are evil, but because incentives are broken.**

Buchanan won the Nobel Prize in 1986.

His insight remains devastating: You can't fix market failure with government failure.

The left still pretends politicians are angels.

Buchanan proved they're just people—with power.

title: Reagan Revives America 1981
body:
## Reagan Revives America 1981

1980 America: 13% inflation, 21% interest rates, 7% unemployment, "malaise," hostages in Iran.

Reagan slashed top tax from 70% to 28%, deregulated energy, airlines, trucking, and backed Volcker's war on inflation.

1983–1989 results:

- 21 million new jobs
- Inflation down to 3%
- Longest peacetime expansion ever
- USSR bankrupted trying to match American growth

"Reaganomics" was classical liberalism on the world stage: lower taxes, sound money, fewer rules = explosive prosperity.

title: Rent-Seeking and Regulatory Capture
body:
## Rent-Seeking and Regulatory Capture

Here's how the game really works:

**Big companies don't fear regulation. They write it.**

Rent-seeking is when businesses use government power to get rich instead of serving customers.

Examples:

- Taxi medallions (blocking Uber to protect incumbents)
- Occupational licensing (requiring 1,000 hours of training to braid hair)
- Sugar tariffs (making Americans pay double to protect sugar producers)
- Banking regulations (so complex only giant banks can comply)

The pattern is always the same:

1. Industry lobbies for "consumer protection"
2. Government creates complex regulations
3. Small competitors can't afford compliance
4. Big companies crush competition legally

This is **regulatory capture**—when regulators serve the industry they're supposed to regulate.

Why does it happen?

- Concentrated benefits (one industry gets rich)
- Dispersed costs (everyone pays a little more)
- Rational ignorance (voters don't notice)

The result: Crony capitalism that enriches the connected and impoverishes everyone else.

Free-market advocates oppose this.

Socialists call it "capitalism" and demand more regulation—which makes it worse.

The solution isn't more government power.

It's less power to capture.

title: Ricardo's Comparative Advantage
body:
## Ricardo's Comparative Advantage

In 1817 David Ricardo published _On the Principles of Political Economy and Taxation_ and proved something that still blows people's minds:

**Even if one country is better at producing everything, both countries still benefit from trade.**

His example:

England produces cloth cheaper than Portugal.

Portugal produces wine cheaper than England.

Easy case—they should trade, right?

But what if Portugal is better at producing _both_ cloth and wine?

Protectionists say: "Portugal should make everything itself!"

Ricardo showed they're wrong.

Even if Portugal is better at both, it should still specialize in whatever it's _most_ better at (wine), and trade for cloth.

Why? Because **opportunity cost** matters more than absolute advantage.

Every hour Portugal spends making cloth is an hour _not_ making wine—where it has an even bigger edge.

The result: Both nations get more of both goods through trade than either could produce alone.

This is **comparative advantage**—the single most counterintuitive, most important, and most ignored insight in all of economics.

It destroys every argument for tariffs, trade wars, and economic nationalism.

Protectionists have spent 200 years pretending Ricardo never existed.

He did. And he was right.

title: Scottish Miracle 1707-1850
body:
## Scottish Miracle 1707-1850

In 1700 Scotland was one of Europe's poorest nations—regular famines, almost no industry, life expectancy under 40.

Then everything changed:

- 1707 Union opened England's markets
- Scottish Enlightenment (Hume, Smith) explained wealth
- Merchants unleashed industry

Result: GDP per person grew **fifteen-fold** in a century—the fastest leap ever recorded. Literacy hit 90%. A nation of shepherds gave the world the steam engine and modern economics.

Capitalism didn't need theories; Scotland lived it.

title: Separation of Powers: Constitutional Genius
body:
## Separation of Powers: Constitutional Genius

The Founders faced an impossible problem:

**How do you create a government strong enough to protect liberty but not strong enough to destroy it?**

Their solution: **Separation of Powers.**

Instead of one sovereign authority, they created three competing branches:

**Legislative Branch** (Congress):

- Makes the laws
- Controls spending
- Can override vetoes
- Can impeach the President

**Executive Branch** (President):

- Enforces the laws
- Commands military
- Can veto legislation
- Appoints judges

**Judicial Branch** (Courts):

- Interprets the laws
- Can strike down unconstitutional acts
- Protects individual rights
- Cannot enforce its own rulings

The genius: Each branch has power, but none has all the power.

They compete. They check each other. They frustrate each other.

This isn't a bug—it's the feature.

Montesquieu warned: "When legislative and executive powers are united in the same person... there can be no liberty."

The Founders listened.

The left wants to eliminate this. They push for:

- Executive orders that bypass Congress
- Court-packing to control the judiciary
- Administrative agencies that make, enforce, and judge their own rules

They call it "progress."

The Founders called it tyranny.

Separation of powers is slow, messy, and frustrating.

It's also the only thing standing between liberty and despotism.

title: Thomas Sowell Dismantles Leftist Myths
body:
## Thomas Sowell Dismantles Leftist Myths

Thomas Sowell has spent 50 years calmly destroying every leftist economic myth with one weapon:

**Facts.**

His books read like intellectual massacres:

**Basic Economics** (2000): Explains supply, demand, and prices so clearly that even politicians could understand—if they wanted to.

**The Vision of the Anointed** (1995): Exposes how elites push policies that make them feel good while making everyone else worse off.

**Discrimination and Disparities** (2018): Proves that group differences in outcomes don't prove discrimination—they prove different choices, cultures, and circumstances.

Sowell's method is devastating:

1. Leftists claim X causes Y
2. Sowell shows the data proves the opposite
3. Leftists ignore him and keep claiming X causes Y

His most famous line:

**"The first lesson of economics is scarcity: there is never enough of anything to fully satisfy all those who want it. The first lesson of politics is to disregard the first lesson of economics."**

Sowell proved you don't need rage or rhetoric.

Just evidence, logic, and the patience to explain why the left is wrong about everything.

title: Spontaneous Order Triumphs
body:
## Spontaneous Order Triumphs

Markets, language, law, money, morals—no central planner designed any of them.

They emerged as spontaneous orders: billions of individual actions, guided by simple rules, producing results no one intended and no one could plan.

Adam Smith's "invisible hand," Hayek's "extended order," Ferguson's "the result of human action, but not of human design."

The pencil you hold: no one on earth knows how to make it from scratch—yet millions cooperate seamlessly through prices and property.

Cities grow without a master architect. The internet exploded without a ministry of information.

Every attempt to replace spontaneous order with conscious design—Soviet five-year plans, Mao's backyard furnaces, Venezuela's price controls—ends in chaos and misery.

Freedom doesn't need a blueprint. It just needs to be left alone.

title: Thatcher Turns Britain 1979
body:
## Thatcher Turns Britain 1979

1979 Britain: 29% inflation, unburied dead, three-day work week, IMF bailout—the "sick man of Europe."

Margaret Thatcher applied classical liberalism with an iron will:

- Sold council houses to tenants
- Broke inflationary union power
- Privatized steel, coal, telecoms, airlines
- Cut top tax rate from 83% to 40%

By 1990: inflation <3%, unemployment falling, GDP growth doubled, home ownership soared.

Britain went from basket case to Europe's strongest economy.

Proof that liberal policies rescue even deeply socialist nations.

title: The Long March Through the Institutions: A Slow-Motion Cultural Coup
grift: The Long March Through the Institutions: A Slow-Motion Cultural Coup
body:
## The Long March Through the Institutions: A Slow-Motion Cultural Coup

After World War II, many European Marxist intellectuals realized that the old dream of a violent proletarian revolution (storming the Winter Palace, guillotine-the-bourgeoisie style) was dead. The working class in the West had been bought off with refrigerators, cars, and Sunday barbecues. Armed uprising wasn’t going to happen.

Enter the German student radical **Rudi Dutschke** in 1967. Borrowing directly from the Italian communist **Antonio Gramsci’s** theories of cultural hegemony, Dutschke popularized the phrase **“the long march through the institutions”**. The plan was simple but diabolical:

> Instead of trying to seize power by force (which would get you crushed by the state), you infiltrate and capture the institutions that shape culture and consciousness: universities, schools, media, publishing houses, churches, the civil service, HR departments, museums, the arts, and eventually even corporate boardrooms.

You do it patiently, generation after generation, by becoming the professors, the journalists, the teachers, the scriptwriters, the diversity officers, the curators. You change the language, rewrite the history books, shift what is considered “normal,” and make your once-radical ideas the new compulsory orthodoxy. Anyone who objects is labeled a bigot, a reactionary, or—today’s favorite—a “fascist.”

The **Frankfurt School** (Marcuse, Adorno, Horkheimer, Habermas, et al.) had already laid the intellectual groundwork in the 1930s–50s, blending Marx with Freud and a deep hatred of Western civilization itself. When they fled Nazi Germany and landed in American universities (especially Columbia), they began the infection in earnest. By the late 60s their students—now tenured—were running entire departments.

## What followed was a fifty-year conquest:

- **1970s**: “Political correctness” starts as an in-joke on the left, then becomes mandatory speech.
- **1980s**: Critical theory metastasizes into Women’s Studies, Black Studies, Queer Studies—entire academic fields built not to pursue truth but to advance ideological warfare.
- **1990s**: The media class graduates from the same universities and brings the new dogma into newsrooms and Hollywood.
- **2000s–2010s**: HR departments and “diversity & inclusion” become the corporate Stasi, enforcing the new secular religion.
- **2020s**: Even kindergarten curricula, library story hours, and scientific bodies are captured. Dissent is not just wrong—it is morally illegitimate, worthy of cancellation, firing, deplatforming.

The result? A civilization that no longer believes in itself, that teaches its children to be ashamed of their history, that polices every word and thought, that replaced individual merit with bureaucratic ideology, and that now struggles to even define “woman” without a 400-page DEI manual.

**The Long March worked.** The revolutionaries never needed rifles. They just needed tenure, microphones, and a lot of patience.

And the scariest part: most people still don’t realize the institutions have already been marched through. They think it’s just “progress.”

**Wake Up NPC, indeed.**

title: Tragedy of the Commons
body:
## Tragedy of the Commons

In 1968 Garrett Hardin published an essay that explained why socialism always fails:

**"The Tragedy of the Commons"**

His example was simple:

Imagine a pasture open to all. Every herder benefits from adding more cattle. But the cost (overgrazing) is shared by everyone.

Result: Each herder adds more cattle until the pasture is destroyed.

**When everyone owns something, no one takes care of it.**

This explains everything:

- Why public parks are trashed but private yards are pristine
- Why government housing projects decay but private homes are maintained
- Why fisheries collapse but cattle ranches thrive
- Why Soviet factories polluted but private companies innovate

The solution isn't "more government."

The solution is **private property rights.**

When someone owns something, they have every incentive to preserve it, improve it, and pass it on.

When everyone owns it, everyone exploits it until it's gone.

Hardin's insight destroys the socialist fantasy:

Collective ownership doesn't create shared prosperity.

It creates shared destruction.

The left still pushes "the commons" as an ideal.

Hardin proved it's a death sentence.

title: Volcker Breaks Inflation 1979-1982
body:
## Volcker Breaks Inflation 1979-1982

By 1979 America was in crisis.

Inflation hit 13%. The dollar was collapsing. Gas lines stretched for blocks. The economy was dying.

Politicians blamed "greed" and "speculators."

Paul Volcker knew better.

In August 1979, President Carter appointed him Fed Chairman. Volcker had one mission:

**Kill inflation. No matter the cost.**

His method was brutal:

Raise interest rates until inflation breaks.

The prime rate hit 21.5%.

Mortgages became unaffordable. Businesses couldn't borrow. Unemployment spiked to 10%.

Farmers drove tractors to the Fed in protest. Homebuilders mailed him 2x4s. Politicians screamed for his head.

Volcker didn't flinch.

He knew the truth: Short-term pain beats long-term destruction.

By 1983, inflation was crushed to 3%.

The economy roared back. Reagan's boom began.

Volcker proved three things:

1. Inflation is always a monetary phenomenon
2. Central banks cause it, and only central banks can stop it
3. Political courage beats political popularity

The left still blames "corporate greed" for inflation.

Volcker proved them wrong 40 years ago.

title: Walter Williams on Minimum Wage
body:
## Walter Williams on Minimum Wage

Walter Williams spent decades explaining one brutal truth:

**The minimum wage destroys jobs for the people it claims to help.**

His argument was simple:

If you're worth $10/hour to an employer, but the law says they must pay you $15/hour, what happens?

You don't get hired.

The minimum wage doesn't raise your value. It just makes you unemployable.

Williams showed the data:

- Before minimum wage laws: black teen unemployment was _lower_ than white teen unemployment
- After minimum wage laws: black teen unemployment skyrocketed to 40%+

Why? Because minimum wage laws price low-skilled workers out of the market.

They can't compete on price, so they can't get the entry-level jobs that teach them skills.

Williams called it what it is:

**"The minimum wage law is one of the most anti-black laws on the books."**

Leftists claim they're helping the poor.

Williams proved they're destroying the first rung of the economic ladder—and laughing while they do it.
